verbatim_text: But still, we have to be guessing the test instance while doing post-training. You know, companies do that through market research or they just have the benchmarks. But, you know, during testing, you still have this fixed model that's passively sort of praying that whatever it sees, right, it needs to be similar to something that it has trained on. Otherwise, all bets are off. So really, for all the past time in machine learning, you know, most of the work focused on training before you see the test instance. So the moment when the test instance arrives, when the user gives a query to your model, is really a defining moment in machine learning. And, you know, after that, people will normally think, you know, testing is pretty boring, right? The interesting part happens before this boundary. And my main argument is that, you know, machine learning in the future is really being trained after you see the test instance. Because that's when you can let go of all the guesswork. Now you say, but like, what can you do at test time? The model is already fixed. You know, pre-training, post-training, all these interesting things. But what if you allow the model ways to be trained at test time?
